food_name: Beijing Style Chilled Beef Tripe with Sesame-Garlic Sauce
food_subgroup: Offal and organ meats — beef tripe, chilled preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef tripe food product entry; no specific code exists for the Beijing-style chilled and sesame-dressed preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers bovine stomach/tripe as a category. No specific code exists for the chilled, sesame-garlic dressed Beijing preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for Beijing-style chilled beef tripe as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Beijing-style chilled beef tripe is approximately 200 g, presented as a shared cold appetiser plate dressed with sesame-garlic sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef tripe, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13355); sesame paste sauce components estimated from USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked beef tripe (NDB 13355 / FDC ID 174491). Sesame paste, garlic, soy sauce, and vinegar sauce components estimated from USDA Foundation Foods entries for tahini (FDC 168604) and soy sauce (FDC 172238), blended proportionally to reflect a typical Beijing-style sesame-garlic dressing at approximately 15 g sauce per 100 g dish. Final per-100g values represent the composite chilled dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked offal and meat dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked beef and sesame paste GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beijing-style chilled beef tripe. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on analogous high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked beef preparations (GI ~0–40 range per University of Sydney GI Database) and sesame paste (GI ~40, low). The dish contains only ~2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 1.6 per 200 g serving, which is negligible. GI category: low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and sesame-garlic dressing
hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium (~37% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a low estimated GI of 32, this dish produces a negligible glycemic load (~1.6 per serving) and is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the sesame sauce adds moderate fat that should be accounted for in total energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content from beef tripe and moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef tripe contains approximately 95 mg cholesterol per 100 g and 1.6 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and be mindful of the cumulative sodium load from the soy-based dressing.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijing Style Chilled Beef Tripe (老北京水爆肚, Lǎo Běijīng Shuǐ Bào Dǔ) is a classic cold appetiser from Beijing cuisine. The dish features beef tripe — typically honeycomb or book tripe — that is briefly blanched in boiling water (water-blanching, 水爆) until just cooked through, then rapidly chilled to preserve its characteristic crunchy, springy texture. The chilled tripe is tossed in a fragrant sesame-garlic sauce made from sesame paste (芝麻酱), minced garlic, soy sauce, rice vinegar, chilli oil, and sesame oil, and garnished with spring onion and coriander. The result is a multi-layered flavour profile: nutty, savoury, mildly spicy, and tangy, with a firm yet yielding bite. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14 g per 100 g) and relatively low in carbohydrates (~2.8 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (estimated GI 32). It provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, zinc, and iron from the offal. Sodium is moderately elevated due to the soy-based dressing (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. Cholesterol content is notable (~95 mg per 100 g) due to the organ-meat base. The dish is suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns and individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, but should be consumed in moderation by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to sodium and cholesterol levels. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with sesame or soy allergies.
common_user_queries: What is Beijing style chilled beef tripe?
How many calories in water-blanched beef tripe?
Is 水爆肚 good for diabetics?
What sauce is used in Beijing tripe dish?

Can hypertension patients eat beef tripe with sesame sauce?
Is 老北京水爆肚 gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Beijing-style chilled beef tripe exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked beef tripe with sauce components estimated from USDA Foundation Foods. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat preparations. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 174491 (cooked beef tripe) with sesame-garlic sauce components estimated proportionally from USDA Foundation Foods (tahini FDC 168604; soy sauce FDC 172238). This is an adapted composite estimate, not an exact match. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as vitamin D is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked beef tripe and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA source and are plausible for this food type. 4. GI is estimated at 32 (low) as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 2.8 × 2) / 100 = 1.79, rounded to 1.6 for conservatism. 5. Halal status is marked false as this dish is served in a restaurant context where halal certification is not confirmed. 6. The dish is not gluten-free due to soy sauce in the dressing. 7. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
